metadata.title: Why the Centrifugal Barrier Is Kinetic Energy Pretending to Be a Force
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = kinetic energy (true origin of centrifugal term); CRIMSON = the centrifugal barrier as it appears on the potential side of the equation (misleading label)
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no spherical coordinate Laplacian derivation, no Laguerre-polynomial solution, no explicit r-dependence of the Laguerre wave functions. Mechanism only: L²/2mr² is angular kinetic energy; after projecting onto eigenstate, it becomes ℏ²ℓ(ℓ+1)/2mr², which migrates to the potential side of the radial equation.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B13: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The centrifugal barrier in the hydrogen radial equation pushes electrons away from the nucleus, sits exactly where a repulsive potential would, and has the shape of a potential. It isn't one.

card.copy: Why the Centrifugal Barrier Is Kinetic Energy Pretending to Be a Force

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. The centrifugal barrier in the hydrogen radial equation pushes electrons away from the nucleus, sits exactly where a repulsive potent

beats.narration_text: In the hydrogen p-orbital, the radial equation has the Coulomb potential on the right side — and sitting right next to it, a term that repels the electron from the nucleus. That term grows with angular momentum. For the s-orbital, it vanishes entirely.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: In the hydrogen p-orbital, the radial equation has the Coulomb potential on the right side — and sitting right next to it, a term that repels the electron from

beats.narration_text: The effective potential for the p-orbital: the Coulomb attraction pulling inward, and the centrifugal barrier pushing outward. Together they create a well with a floor above the pure Coulomb minimum. The electron orbits at a larger radius than the s-state.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The effective potential for the p-orbital: the Coulomb attraction pulling inward, and the centrifugal barrier pushing outward. Together they create a well with

beats.narration_text: The Question: the centrifugal term repels the electron from the nucleus the way a potential would. It lives on the potential side of the equation. But it came from the angular kinetic energy. Is it kinetic energy or a force?

card.copy: The centrifugal term repels like a potential. But it came from L²/2mr². Kinetic or force?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The Question: the centrifugal term repels the electron from the nucleus the way a potential would. It lives on the potential side of the equation. But it came f

beats.narration_text: Naive reading: the centrifugal term sits on the right side of the equation, next to V of r. That is where potentials live. It repels, the way potentials can. The label says centrifugal force. Must be a force.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Naive reading: the centrifugal term sits on the right side of the equation, next to V of r. That is where potentials live. It repels, the way potentials can. Th

beats.narration_text: The mechanism: the full 3D kinetic energy operator splits into a radial part and an angular part — L-squared over 2-m-r-squared. That is angular kinetic energy. It lives on the kinetic side of the Schrödinger equation, not the potential side.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The mechanism: the full 3D kinetic energy operator splits into a radial part and an angular part — L-squared over 2-m-r-squared. That is angular kinetic energy.

beats.narration_text: Project onto a state with angular momentum quantum number ell. The operator L-squared becomes its eigenvalue, h-bar squared times ell times ell-plus-one. That fixed number — divided by 2-m-r-squared — is still kinetic energy, but it now looks like a position-dependent number.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Project onto a state with angular momentum quantum number ell. The operator L-squared becomes its eigenvalue, h-bar squared times ell times ell-plus-one. That f

beats.narration_text: When we rearrange the radial equation, that kinetic term migrates to the right side — the same side as V of r. It looks like a potential. It was never a force. The repulsion is the cost of spinning: if the electron gets close to the nucleus it needs more angular kinetic energy, and the total energy budget doesn't allow it.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: When we rearrange the radial equation, that kinetic term migrates to the right side — the same side as V of r. It looks like a potential. It was never a force.

beats.narration_text: The implication: the integer ell controls the centrifugal barrier strength. Bigger ell means more rotation, more angular kinetic energy at small r, a higher floor in the effective potential, and a larger characteristic orbital radius. Centrifugal repulsion is always kinetic.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The implication: the integer ell controls the centrifugal barrier strength. Bigger ell means more rotation, more angular kinetic energy at small r, a higher flo

beats.narration_text: A student draws the effective potential for ell equals one and labels the centrifugal term as repulsive force. Her instructor asks her to trace the term back through the separation of variables. She finds it in the kinetic-energy operator — L-squared over 2-m-r-squared — and rewrites the label: angular kinetic energy. No force involved.
beats.example_note: illustrative — directly from the card's example seed

beats.narration_text: Why does the centrifugal barrier repel without being a force? The kinetic energy operator in 3D has an angular piece, L-squared over 2-m-r-squared. In an angular-momentum eigenstate, that becomes a number that migrates to the potential side of the radial equation. It looks like V of r. It is still kinetic energy.

card.copy: L²/2mr² = angular kinetic energy.  Projected onto eigenstate → ℏ²ℓ(ℓ+1)/2mr².  Migrates to V-side.  Still kinetic.
